old_claim.note: Printed Map Atlases provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Copperplate map engraving was designed to transfer inked intaglio lines repeatedly onto paper as part of the Renaissance print-production system.

source_shape.source_locator: Pages 3 and 10 explain copperplate intaglio map printing and identify the 1478 Rome Ptolemy's Geography as using punched lettering throughout its engraved maps.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The cartography chapter explains how engraved intaglio plates transferred inked map lines repeatedly onto paper.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Printing is the production capability used by the engraved plate, while an atlas is a later compiled publication that can consume engraved maps.

ontology_before: precision_map_engraving inherited from printed_map_atlases, which the cited evidence did not establish as its direct enabling method.
ontology_after: precision_map_engraving inherits from printing_press, the source-documented capability used by the scoped milestone.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: printing_press replaces printed_map_atlases as a direct prerequisite for precision_map_engraving.
would_reject_if: Direct evidence showed that printed_map_atlases, rather than printing_press, supplied the operative method used in the scoped precision_map_engraving milestone.
short_reason: Copperplate printing produces maps and predates the compiled atlas milestone.
